coredata.dc:description: Iron is one of the essential trace elements in the human body, which is involved in many important physiological processes of life. The abnormal amount of iron in the body will bring many diseases. Therefore, a novel near-infrared fluorimetric method was developed. The method is based on a fluorescent probe (E)-4-(2-(3-(dicyanomethylene)-5,5-dimethylcyclohex-1-en-1-yl)vinyl)-N, N-diethylaniline oxide (DDED) which uses N-oxide as a recognition group to real-time monitoring and imaging of Fe2+ in vivo and in vitro. The method exhibits excellent selectivity and high sensitivity (LOD = 27 nM) for Fe2+, fast reaction rate (< 4 min), extremely large Stokes shift (＞ 275 nm), low cytotoxicity. The strip test strongly illustrates the potential application of DDED in real environment. In particular, DDED has been successfully applied to real-time monitoring and imaging of Fe2+ in HepG2 cells and zebrafish. That is, the method has great potential for the detection of Fe2+ in living systems.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron is one of the essential trace elements in the human body, which is involved in many important physiological processes of life. The abnormal amount of iron in the body will bring many diseases. Therefore, a novel near-infrared fluorimetric method was developed. The method is based on a fluorescent probe (E)-4-(2-(3-(dicyanomethylene)-5,5-dimethylcyclohex-1-en-1-yl)vinyl)-N, N-diethylaniline oxide (DDED) which uses N-oxide as a recognition group to real-time monitoring and imaging of Fe2+ in vivo and in vitro. The method exhibits excellent selectivity and high sensitivity (LOD = 27 nM) for Fe2+, fast reaction rate (< 4 min), extremely large Stokes shift (＞ 275 nm), low cytotoxicity. The strip test strongly illustrates the potential application of DDED in real environment. In particular, DDED has been successfully applied to real-time monitoring and imaging of Fe2+ in HepG2 cells and zebrafish. That is, the method has great potential for the detection of Fe2+ in living systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is one of the metal elements with the highest content in the earth’s crust, which is also closely related to human life (Zhu et al., 2019a, 2019b, 2019c, 2019d, 2019e; Ni et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020). Iron is a necessary element for many life activities in most organisms (Kumar et al., 2014). Many cell-level chemical and biological processes require the participation of iron ions (Maiti et al., 2015; Sakamoto et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2019). It also plays a key role in transporting nutrients and oxygen in the blood (Qu et al., 2016). The lack of iron in our body will result in iron-deficiency anemia, manifested by chlorosis of the face, lack of blood color on the skin, which may lead to metabolic disorders and decreased immune function, thereby inducing various diseases (Sen et al., 2012). The iron element usually exists in the form of divalent iron and ferric iron (Zeng et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2020). Divalent iron has both reducing property and oxidizing property, which is conducive to rapid and complex metabolic activities in the organism and is necessary for the synthesis of hemoglobin (Hirayama and Nagasawa, 2017). Besides, iron is also an important part of nitrogenase (Senthilkumar et al., 2016). However, excess iron ions in the body will cause serious health problems, which also may give rise to cancer and aging of the heart, pancreas and liver (Finelli et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017). Not only that, the abnormal level of iron could also be involved in neurological diseases such as Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s (Wei et al., 2020; Sen et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2018). Due to the existence of a reducing environment and the poor solubility of Fe3+ at a neutral and weakly alkaline physiological pH, Fe3+ in the cell is easily reduced to Fe2+. And the free Fe2+ can catalyze the Fenton reaction, leading to the generation of free radicals (Kim et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020; Malik et al., 2018). Therefore, the amount of free Fe2+ in the cell must be controlled at the lowest level of physiological needs (Zheng et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020).

In addition, the low valent iron ions are also widely used in wastewater treatment. The reduction system involved can completely reduce and degrade pollutants in the wastewater and repair the damaged environment effectively. Unfortunately, the treated industrial sewage containing excessive iron will be discharged into the natural environment and may be ingested directly or indirectly by the human body, which can cause the occurrence of diseases (Bousalah et al., 2020; Wen et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Yuan et al., 2019). Therefore, it is very necessary to do the research and develop effective detection methods of iron ions with high sensitivity, low detection limit and good selectivity.

Current methods for detecting iron ions include flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS), graphite furnace flame atomic absorption spectrometry (GFAAS), inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), electrochemical method, colorimetric method, etc (Wu et al., 2019; Long et al., 2018; Tavoli and Alizadeh, 2016; Dhivya et al., 2019). Although these detection methods have high sensitivity, low detection limit and wide linear range, FAAS, GFAAS and ICP-AES can only measure total iron content and cannot conduct valence state analysis (X. Zhang et al., 2020; RQ. Zhang et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2019). ICP-MS can conduct valence state analysis, but the instrument is expensive (Zhang et al., 2016). As an emerging fluorescence analysis method, organic fluorescent probe has the advantages of simple and convenient, low cost, simple instrument operation, high selectivity and high sensitivity compared with traditional instrument detection methods (Zhu et al., 2018). Most fluorescent probes have their own limitations, which are manifested in the differences of structure and morphology. Therefore, the emission wavelength of most fluorescent probes is limited to 650 nm. However, the near-infrared fluorescent probe has a very good improvement in this aspect. The emission wavelengths of near-infrared fluorescent probes are all over 650 nm, which can avoid the interference of autofluorescence of the sample, thereby making the chemical analysis of fluorescence and biological fluorescence imaging more accurate. Near-infrared fluorescence detection methods are widely used in nucleic acid, protein, bioimaging, immunoassay and other fields, showing great potential in biological analysis (Zhang et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020; He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020). In addition, the establishment of near-infrared fluorimetric method also have shortcomings, such as a few types of dyes, expensive raw materials, difficulty in synthesis, and unstable light, heat and chemical properties of dyes (Liu et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2020). Therefore, it is imminent to develop a kind of near infrared fluorescent probe with stable chemical structure, simple synthesis steps and cheap raw materials.

In recent years, many kinds of iron ion fluorescent probes have been reported, such as iron ion fluorescent probes based on rhodamine, Schiff base, coumarin, fluorescein, crown ether, imidazole, leptin, naphthalimide and other fluorescent structures (Zhu et al., 2019a, 2019b, 2019c, 2019d, 2019e; Jia et al., 2015, 2018, 2020; Ni et al., 2020; He et al., 2021). Unfortunately, most fluorescent probes are designed on the basis of Fe2+ coordination. The response to Fe2+ usually shows fluorescence quenching during the detection process. However, this phenomenon cannot be used to accurately identify and detect Fe2+ (Spiliopoulos, 2019; Shi et al., 2017; Sharov et al., 1995). There is still a lack of practical Fe2+ fluorescent probes that can overcome low water solubility and specificity (Santhoshkumar et al., 2016; Rattanopas et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019a, 2019b, 2019c, 2019d, 2019e). Due to the influence of fluorescence quenching on detection, it is necessary to design the probes that can react with Fe2+ and release fluorescence. Thus far, the design of novel water-soluble Fe2+ probe has always been the focus of research. There are still many challenges in developing novel Fe2+ fluorescent probe molecules with simple molecular mechanism, good water solubility, high selectivity and sensitivity.

In this work, in order to establish a simple, highly selective and sensitive near-infrared fluorescence method, we reported a near-infrared fluorescent probe with extremely large Stokes shift (＞ 275 nm) based on the N-oxide chemistry (E)-4-(2-(3-(dicyanomethylene)-5,5- dimethylcyclohex-1-en-1-yl)vinyl)-N, N-diethylaniline oxide (DDED), which can be used to effectively detect and visualize Fe2+ in aqueous solutions, living cells, and organisms. Interestingly, the method shows the advantages of fast near-infrared fluorescence response (< 4 min), low detection limit (27 nM), and low cytotoxicity. Not only that, the probe also has a 230-fold fluorescence enhancement at 675 nm. Notably, the probe exhibits an excellent near-infrared fluorescence bioimaging capability for Fe2+ in living cells and zebrafish, indicating that the method has broad application prospects in the visualization of Fe2+ in living organisms.
(E)-2-(3-(4- (diethylamino)styryl)-5,5-dimethylcyclohex-2-en-1-ylidene) malononitrile (DDPI) was synthesized according to the reported method (Yang et al., 2019).

3-chloroperbenzoic acid (m-CPBA, 750 mg, 4.34 mmol) was added to CH2Cl2 (50 mL) containing DDPI (1.0 g, 2.89 mmol) under ice bath conditions. The mixture was then stirred at room temperature for 4 h until the reaction was completed. Thereafter, the reaction mixture was concentrated and purified with a silica gel column (VDCM/VMethanol = 4:1) to obtain DDED as the yellow powder (135 mg, 75%). Melting point: 170.8 ℃. Water solubility: 22.86 mg/L. 1H NMR (d6-DMSO, 600 Hz) δ: 7.50 (d, J = 8.5 Hz, 2 H), 7.20 (d, J = 15.9 Hz, 1 H), 7.06 (d, J = 15.8 Hz, 1 H), 6.70 (s, 1 H), 6.66 (d, J = 8.5 Hz, 2 H), 3.38 (q, J = 7.0 Hz, 4 H), 2.55 (s, 2 H), 2.50 (s, 2 H), 1.09 (t, J = 7.0 Hz, 6 H), 0.99 (s, 6 H). 13C NMR (d6-DMSO, 151 MHz) δ: 170.23, 157.93, 149.43, 139.93, 130.66, 124.05, 123.19, 120.37, 115.01, 114.20, 111.86, 73.04, 44.24, 42.83, 40.59, 39.69, 38.80, 32.07, 27.93, 12.96. FT-IR (KBr, cm−1): 3425.61, 2957.27, 2220.46, 1617.67, 1563.61, 1527.75, 1466.91, 1370.43, 1325.78, 1200.27, 1136.22, 995.26, 959.80, 863.49, 701.72, 591.33. HRMS (ESI, m/z) Calcd for [C23H27N3O + H]+: 362.2227, found: 362.2228.

The DDED probe was dissolved in DMSO to formulate a 10 mM stock solution. Fe2+ and analytes (FeCl20.4 H2O, Mg(NO3)20.6 H2O, Zn(NO3)20.6 H2O, HgCl2, Na2SO4, Fe(NO3)30.9 H2O, CoCl3, Ca(NO3)20.4 H2O, NaOH, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, AgNO3, NaNO3, Cu(NO3)20.3 H2O, KNO3, NiCl2 and AgNO3) were prepared into a 5 × 10−3 M solution. The conditions of fluorescence spectra were optimized (E x = 400 nm, E m = 550–900 nm, and slit width: 10 nm).

Normally growing HepG2 cells were selected for cytotoxicity test. HepG2 cells were cultured in a 96-well cell culture plate for 24 h at 37 ℃, and then incubated in medium containing different concentrations of DDED (0, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 μM) for another 24 h. The 5% freshly prepared MTT (3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)- 2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromides) solution was added to incubate with HepG2 cells for 4 h and removed the supernatant. Then, DMSO (150 μL) was added to each well, and the absorbance at 490 nm was measured with a microplate reader, and the cell survival rate was obtained by calculating the ratio of the absorbance to the blank control. Calculated as follows:

control is the average absorbance of untreated HepG2 cells, and n is the number of parallels for each group of experiments. C x is the absorbance after treatment with series of concentration gradients of DDED (x = 0, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 μM).

HepG2 cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 10 IU/mL penicillin and 10 μg/mL streptomycin. The cell culture environment contains 5% CO2, 95% air, and a temperature of 37 °C. The DDED solution was added to 1 mL of medium containing cells (final concentration of DDED was 20 μM) and incubated at 37 ℃ for 4 h. Then it was washed three times with Tris-HCl (20 mM, pH 7.4) buffer, and incubated in a medium containing Fe2+ for 30 min. HepG2 cell samples incubated with different concentrations of Fe2+ were obtained for imaging under a fluorescence microscope (E x = 520 nm, Olympus, Japan).

Normal 3-day-old zebrafish were selected for imaging experiments. The experiment is divided into three groups, blank group a (zebrafish without any treatment), experimental group b was zebrafish treated with DDED only, and experimental group c was zebrafish treated with both DDED and Fe2+. Specifically, the zebrafish were sequentially transferred to the culture beaker. First, probe DDED (30 μM) was added to the culture beaker and incubated for 30 min. Then, Fe2+ (50 μM) was added and the incubation was continued for 30 min. Finally, it was imaged on a fluorescence microscope (E x = 520 nm, E m = 650–750 nm, Olympus, BX43-32P02-FLB3, Japan).

Due to the superior fluorescence characteristics, near-infrared emission and high fluorescence quantum yield of DDPI, it was chosen as the fluorophore of the probe DDED. Also, the N-diethylamino group of DDPI was easily oxidized to N-oxide with high yield (82%). The entire reaction process not only blocks ICT and quenches its fluorescence, but is also based on N-oxide chemistry which provides a specific reaction site for Fe2+. The hydrogen atoms on the dimethyl group and the two methylene groups of dicyanoisophorone group for DDED are located at 0.99 ppm (6H) and 2.50–2.55 ppm (4H). The chemical shifts of the hydrogen atom on the diethylamino group are 1.09 ppm (6H) and 3.38 (4H), respectively (Figs. S1 and 2). The substance with a molecular weight of 362.2228 was found in the high resolution mass spectrum, which corresponds to the calculated molecular weight of DDED (m/z: 362.2227). Not only that, the FT-IR spectrum of DDED shows a characteristic absorption band (3423.61 cm−1) related to intermolecular hydrogen bonds. The peaks at 2957.27 cm−1 and 2220.46 cm−1 correspond to C-H stretching and C≡N stretching vibrations, respectively. The appearance of peaks at 1617.67 and 1563.61 cm−1 are caused by the stretching of C˭C in the conjugated structure. The characteristic absorption band of N‒O tensile vibration shows the presence of N-oxide at 1466.91 cm−1 (Fig. S4). That is, an ICT-based near-infrared emission (NIR) fluorescent probe DDED with extremely large Stokes shift was successfully prepared by introducing the target site N-oxide into DDPI ( Scheme 1).

The detection environment of the fluorescent probe was very important in the process of recognizing the target molecule. In particular, when the probe molecule recognizes the metal ion, the solvent effect, pH, excitation band range and reaction time will directly affect the recognition performance of the probe. Based on this, we optimized the reaction conditions of the probe DDED and Fe2+. Under the same conditions, different solvents (EtOH, methanol, DMSO, n-hexane, PE, acetonitrile, DMF, CH2Cl2, THF, EA) were selected to determine the solvent-dependent of the probe to detect Fe2+. Among many solvents, DMSO enables DDED to have higher sensitivity and longer maximum emission wavelength, so DMSO was chosen as the standard solvent for subsequent experiments ( Fig. 1a). On this basis, the effects of different ratios of the DMSO-PBS system on the fluorescence properties of DDED were studied. It can be seen from Fig. 1(a) inset that 40% of DMSO can maximize the fluorescence characteristics of DDED.

The excitation wavelength of the detection system is also one of the factors that hinder the study of the sensitivity of DDED to Fe2+. Based on this, we verified the optimal excitation wavelength of the DDED-Fe2+ system (Fig. 1b). Although the maximum emission wavelength of the DDED-Fe2+ system was greater than other excitation wavelengths when the excitation wavelength was 380 nm, the fluorescence enhancement amplitude is lower. Therefore, 400 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength of the DDED-Fe2+ system under comprehensive consideration.

The pH value has an obvious influence on the existing form of Fe2+, which in turn affects the fluorescence emission intensity of the DDED-Fe2+ system. Therefore, the effect of the pH value on the fluorescence intensity of the DDED and DDED-Fe2+ systems was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the fluorescence intensity of DDED-Fe2+ system is on a rise with the increase of pH in the range of 2–7.4. On the contrary, when DDED-Fe2+ system solution is alkaline, the fluorescence intensity of the DDED-Fe2+ system decreases with the increase of the pH. Therefore, physiological pH 7.4 was selected because the subsequent experiments will be carried out in cells and biological tissues.

The reaction time between probe and target ion is an important index to investigate the fluorescence properties of probe. After adding Fe2+ (20 μM) to DDED (10 μM), the fluorescence intensity of DDED increased rapidly and reached equilibrium within 4 min. Not only that, the calculated pseudo-first-order reaction rate constant (K′) of the DDED-Fe2+ system was (1.12 ± 0.01) × 10−2 s−1. The results show that DDED has high sensitivity to Fe2+. The rapid and stable response of DDED to Fe2+ and the resulting rapid response characteristics were an important basis of real-time detection of Fe2+. Compared with previous reports (Table S1), the rapid identification feature of DDED was more advantageous in the detection of Fe2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and anti-interference of DDED for Fe2+
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent molecular probes with excellent performance should perform high-efficiency and specific detection on the recognition target, so it is our first priority to investigate the fluorescence selectivity of the probe DDED to various analytes. The probe solution itself was light yellow in the solution whose test system is DMSO-PBS (2:3, v/v, pH 7.4), and it is found that the probe itself has no obvious fluorescence emission (ϕ＜0.001) by the fluorescence test. It indicates that the ICT process of DDED is inhibited. However, as Fe2+ (50 μM）was added to the DDED solution, a strong emission peak appeared at 675 nm was clearly observed (230-fold enhancement on the fluorescent intensity, ϕ=0.62), and the color of the solution changed to red. Then we added other analytes of the same multiple to the probe solution for testing. As shown in Fig. 2(a), only Fe2+ would cause a strong fluorescence enhancement. The fluorescence change of the solution under 365 nm UV lamp also verified the above results (no fluorescence to red fluorescence). That is, the probe DDED has a good recognition effect on Fe2+.

It is proverbial that the interference experiment of other metal ions on the probe recognition process was also one of the indicators to measure whether this probe can become an efficient probe, so we conducted a titration competition experiment. Different analytes were added to the probe solution, as shown in Fig. 2(b), other analytes did not make the probe DDED solution have obvious fluorescence changes except for Fe2+ (Black bars). After adding Fe2+ (50 μM) in turn, the fluorescence of all solutions was significantly enhanced. This demonstrates that DDED recognizes Fe2+ (50 μM) without interference from other analytes (red bars). Therefore, it can be used as a highly selective recognition fluorescent probe for practical application. See Fig. 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the spectral performance of DDED
ce:section.ce:para: Stokes shift was one of the important parameters for evaluating the photophysical properties of fluorescent probe. As depicted in Fig. 3, the UV absorption spectrum of DDED itself has only one peak at 380 nm, and after adding Fe2+, another absorption peak appears at 510 nm. The maximum emission wavelengths of the DDED and DDED-Fe2+ systems were both 675 nm. Based on the Stokes shift calculation formula, the Stoke shifts of the DDED and DDED-Fe2+ systems were 295 nm and 165 nm, respectively. Compared with the previously reported fluorescent probes, the extremely large Stokes shift of DDED reduces the test error caused by fluorescence self-quenching and Rayleigh scattering. Therefore, it has a better signal-to-noise ratio when performing biological imaging in cells and living bodies.

To verify the fluorescence response of DDED to Fe2+, fluorescence and UV–vis titration experiments were carried out to evaluate the sensitivity of DDED to Fe2+ ( Fig. 4). Fig. 4(b) shows the absorbance of DDED-Fe2+ system increases with the increase of Fe2+. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity at 675 nm was enhanced as the concentration of Fe2+ increases from 0 to 50 μM (Fig. 4a). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of DDED at 690 nm has an excellent linear correlation with the concentration of Fe2+ (R2 = 0.9997) (Fig. 4 inset). The calculated detection limit (LOD, 3σ/slope) of Fe2+ was 27 nM, which also represents that DDED exhibits high sensitivity to Fe2+.

In order to ascertain the response mechanism of the probe DDED and Fe2+, the high-resolution mass spectrum of the probe DDED-Fe2+ system was determined. A new peak was found at m/z 346.2278 in the mass spectrogram of the reaction solution of DDED and Fe2+, corresponding to the reduction product DDPI (calculated value 346.2278) ( Fig. 5). Obviously, DDPI is a large π-conjugated system with an obvious push-pull structure. DDED molecule contains N-oxide electron donor (push) and (E)-2-(3-(4-(diethylamino)styryl)- 5,5-dimethylcyclohex-2-en-1-ylidene) malononitrile electron acceptor (pull). When DDED reacts with Fe2+, the potential electron donor is activated, so a new push-pull conjugate system is formed in the molecule, which forms the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process. The results confirmed that the reduction of the probe DDED to DDPI caused ICT enhancement and induced strong red fluorescence.

The changes of functional group during the reaction between DDED and Fe2+ were confirmed by FT-IR spectrum. The FT-IR spectrum of DDED shows the band related to intermolecular hydrogen bonding at 3477.99 cm−1. At the same time, the peaks at 3004.07 cm−1 and 2228.34 cm−1 correspond to the stretching of C-H and the stretching vibration of C≡N, respectively. The appearance of peak at 1679.01 cm−1 was caused by the stretching of C˭C in the conjugated structure. The characteristic absorption band of N-O stretching vibration shows the presence of N-oxide at 1496.01 cm−1. When DDED reacts with Fe2+, the characteristic absorption band of N‒O disappears, while other characteristic peaks did not change. The results of FT-IR spectroscopy further verify that Fe2+ reacts with N-oxide on DDED and breaks N‒O, resulting in the fluorescence.

The chemical compositions of DDED and DDED-Fe2+ system were analyzed by XPS spectrum (Fig. S5). Three peaks were observed at C1s 284.8 eV, O1s 531.2 eV and N1s 399.3 eV with relative atomic percentages of 81.1%, 9.26% and 9.65%, respectively (Fig. S5). The results show that DDED is mainly composed of C, N and O. The high resolution C1s spectrum (Figure. S5b) can be divided into three peaks of 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV and 288.6 eV, which are assigned to the C‒C, C˭C and C≡N groups, respectively. The two different states of nitrogen in the N1s spectrum (Fig. S5) were recorded at 399.3 eV and 403.1 eV, corresponding to C≡N and C‒N‒O, respectively (Figure. S5c). In the DDED-Fe2+ system, 403.1 eV representing N‒O disappeared, and other peaks remained unchanged (Fig. S6). The XPS spectrum is consistent with the above conclusion, that is, Fe2+ reacts with O atoms on DDED to break N‒O and release DDPI. See Fig. 6.

Energy and geometric characteristics were calculated by Gaussian 09 W. The geometric configurations of DDPI and DDED were obtained by using 6-31G* basis group optimization at the B3LYP theoretical level through the method of density universal function calculation. The molecular orbital (MO) analysis of DDPI and DDED shows that the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) on the fluorophore were well localized. As shown in Figs. S7 and 8, the optimized fluorophores of DDED and DDPI have flexible planar properties. In order to visualize the electronic structure of these compounds, we calculated the electron density map ( Fig. 7) for DDPI and DDED using B3LYP/6-31G*, which visually shows the electron cloud distribution of each part of the molecule. The color changes indicate different electron densities, with red showing electron abundance and blue showing electron deficiency. The LUMO of DDPI was dispersed on other molecular sites except N, N-diethylaniline oxide, and the HOMO was mainly on the benzene ring site of N, N-diethylaniline oxide. LUMO of DDED was mainly distributed on the fluorophore, while its HOMO is distributed on the N, N-diethylaniline oxide site. With the reaction with Fe2+, HOMO transfers to the fluorophore as a whole, inducing enhanced fluorescence of the DDED-Fe2+ system, which was consistent with the conclusion drawn by the above HRMS. The excitation light would cause a directed charge transfer from donor to acceptor in DDED. When the recognition group of DDED interacts with Fe2+, the electron donating ability of the electron donor changes, which changes the charge distribution in the molecule, affects the conjugation degree of the electron push-pull system and causes a change in the energy level difference. Theoretically, the HUMO-LUMO band gap value (ΔE) would also determine the fluorescence properties of the molecule. Through simulation calculations, the ΔE value of DDED and DDPI was 1.98 eV and 2.31 eV, respectively. Compared with DDPI, DDED is more prone to electronic transitions. The results showed that Fe2+ restored N-oxide in DDED and released fluoro (E)-2-(4- (dilamino)styryl)-5,5- dimethylcyclohex-2-en-1-ylidene) malononitrile, which turned on ICT process to induce fluorescence enhancement.

The study of practical application value of probes is very necessary for the future development of probes. Therefore, we carried out the addition and recovery experiment of DDED to Fe2+. To ensure the wide adaptability of the test results, different water samples were collected from different environmental water bodies. And before the detection, the filter was processed by the micro-filtration membrane to avoid unnecessary interference. The concentration of Fe2+ in environmental water samples had a good linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity of DDED solution (R2 =0.9961, Fig. S9). The results showed that exogenous Fe2+ was detected from environmental water samples, and the recovery rate was between 88% and 104% ( Table 1). That is, DDED has the potential to detect Fe2+ in environmental water samples. See Fig. 8.

Test strips were more attractive in practical application due to the advantages of simple production, rapid detection and convenient use. DDED solution (20 μM) was prepared with CH2Cl2. The filter paper was immersed in air and dried in the air. Then, the aqueous solutions containing different concentrations of Fe2+ were added to the test paper, and the color and fluorescence changes were observed under the naked eye and UV–vis lamp (365 nm) after natural drying. It can be seen from Fig. 8(a) that the DDED test strip has no fluorescence under 365 nm UV–vis lamp, and with the continuous addition of Fe2+, the test strip changes from no fluorescence to red fluorescence, and its fluorescence intensity enhance with the increase of the concentration of Fe2+. Furthermore, the test strip containing DDED itself was light yellow. As the concentration of Fe2+ continues to increase, the test strip gradually changes from light yellow to pink and deepen continuously (Fig. 8b). The results show that DDED had great potential in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of Fe2+ in living HepG2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of DDED is a primary concern before the application of DDED in biological cells and living tissues are explored. MTT colorimetry was used to detect the survival rate of HepG2 cells with different concentrations of DDED within 24 h ( Fig. 9). Although the concentration of DDED reached to 50 μM, the survival rate of HepG2 cells was also above 87%, which indicates that the cytotoxicity of probe DDED was very low.

To explore the response of probe DDED to Fe2+ in living cells, HepG2 cells were selected for imaging experiments with endogenous and exogenous Fe2+ (Fig. 9a). HepG2 cells that were not treated and treated with exogenous Fe2+ did not show any fluorescence enhancement. When treated only with DDED, the HepG2 cells emit weak red fluorescence because of the endogenous Fe2+ in HepG2 cells reacts with the probe DDED to release fluorophores. However, HepG2 cells treated with exogenous Fe2+ (50 μM) and DDED showed strong red fluorescence, which was due to the addition of exogenous Fe2+ increased the concentration of Fe2+ in the cells, and then reacted with DDED to release more fluorescent groups. More interestingly, the intracellular red fluorescence gradually increased with the increase of the concentration of Fe2+ (Fig. 9c). Moreover, the red fluorescence in the cells became more and more intense with the increase of time, reaching its maximum value after about 30 min (Fig. S10). In conclusion, DDED can selectively detect and visualize endogenous and exogenous Fe2+ in HepG2 cells.

The similarity between zebrafish gene and human gene is 87%, which means the results obtained from drug experiments on zebrafish may represent those from human body in most cases. On the basis of cell imaging, we evaluated the fluorescence imaging effect of Fe2+ in living vertebrate zebrafish ( Fig. 10). The untreated and DDED-treated zebrafish did not show any fluorescence under fluorescence microscope. In the presence of DDED and Fe2+ (50 μM), strong red fluorescence could be clearly observed, which mainly distributed in the yolk sac and digestive tract, including the intestinal and biliary system (gallbladder, liver, pancreas, single islet and bile duct). In addition, the product of DDED reacted with Fe2+ was stable in zebrafish, and no fluorescence weakening was observed in at least 1 h. The results showed that DDED could enter the zebrafish easily and real-time monitor the distribution of Fe2+ in zebrafish.

In summary, a fluorescence-based method for point-of-care monitoring of Fe2+ was developed. The method exhibits excellent Fe2+ sensing performance, including low detection limit (27 nm), fast response (< 4 min), high selectivity and sensitivity. The detection mechanism of DDED with Fe2+ was verified by HRMS and DFT. In addition, extremely large Stokes shift (＞ 275 nm) of DDED enhanced its penetration of cells, tissues, and in vivo, and reduced background fluorescence interference. The strip test strongly illustrates the practical application potential of the method. More importantly, the method can visually monitor the distribution of Fe2+ in cells and zebrafish. The design concept of this fluorescent probe could provide theoretical support for the future development of targeted Fe2+ fluorescent probes.
